repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Ethnomusicology Archive
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Archive of California and Western Folklore
description_tesim: Recorded by Colin D. Edwards at UCLA for CBC Studios in Toronto

"Witchcraft" - a sound encyclopedia composed of a conversation with Dr. Wayland d. Hand, Professor  of German and Folklore, University of California, Los Angeles, conducted by Colin D. Edwardsfirst section (29 min.30sec)

Side I Origin and antiquity of Witchcraft Similarities and differences: witch, witch doctor, medicine man Self-consciousness of witches - people in the community decide who is a witch Role in community - isolation and difference Witches' powers Image of witch - Germanic Europe in particular - physically Witches stake becomes broomstick Burning at the stake: ability to change physical form (usually into animal form), witches' familiar Witch-riding - witch rides human beings during the night, 1890 actual case involving witch-riding, this can also happen to animals Ruination of fields Witch/Wizard distinction - why no male witches Becoming a witch: acts of sacriligious nature, pact with the devil; physical and spiritual, witches' sabbaths and initiation rites Institutionalized witchcraft - 1489 start Encyclopedia of Witchcraft - published by 2 theologians Married and unmarried women as witches Methods of counteracting witches Methods of counteracting witches: the use of iron and steel fastened about entrances to the premises such as a horseshoe over the door, a sieve or flour sifter near keyhole, chimney, window, scissors - particularly open in the form of a Christian cross, plants; all hardwood protects against witches, oak, ash, beech, birch, maple, hickory, which hazel, mustard seed in Latin America because she has to count the seeds and witches are bad counters, when witches work at night steal their skin and salt it with red pepper. When they put their skin back on they will burn to death, shoot witch in effigy with a silver bullet to kill Werewolf/witch similarities: change or turn skin - creatures who can get out of their skin Sacred objects (esp. Bible) used to combat witches Household objects used to combat witches

description_tesim: Side I An old miner named Sam - Delta Material in Dear Old Georgia. Jugo-Slavian Peasant Songs and Dances: Hop Saj Sasa - Croatia; Oj Mladosti Naj Milise Doba - Croatia; Sano Duso (sub-title: Vranjanka) - Serbia (Folkdancer records MH 3020; 78,B); Cacak - Serbia (Folkdancer Records MH 3022; 78,B); Krici Krici Ticek - Croatia; Poskakusa - Croatia; Kolo Kalendara - Croatia; Dance from Pirot - Servia (Sto Mi Omilelo); Makedonka - Serbia (Balkan 547; 78,A); Cujes Mala - Serbia; Kokonjeste - Serbia; Dajchovoto - Serbia; Serbian Medley: Poskok, Ti Momo, Djurdjevka, Igrale Se Delije. Side II Strike Music of American Labor: Hallelujah I'm a Bum (Greenway?); Pie in the Sky; Solidarity Forever; Union Maid - (a dub of the Almanac Singers record); Talking Union - (a dub of the Almanac Singers record); Sister Jenny's Turn to Throw the Ball; Ludlow Massacre - Woody Guthrie; Which Side - Almanacs; Join the NMU; Harry Sims; Fight for Union Recognition (tune: I've been workig on the railroad); The Boatmakers' Union is a No Good Union. Jane Fitzpatrick: An Ould Mon Came A-Courtin'; Oh Dear, What Shall Ee Dae; The Drowned Lover; Unnamed Tune.
Title from tape: Allen * Weide * Fitzpatrick
Performers: Jane Fitzpatrick, Sam (miner, last name unknown).

description_tesim: Collected by: Mrs. Anne M. Evans

1. The Micmac Indians. Relations with her father;2. Molly Knockwood prophecies birth of Mrs. Carey;3. Molly Knockwood prophecies that Mrs. Carey will never be drowned. Mrs. Carey given an Indian name. Instances where Mrs. Carey was saved from drowning;4. Indians' ability to prophecy;5. How the other whites hated Indians;6. Taboo against Indians having doorsteps;7. Indian charm for conceiving children. Its use by whites;8. Her mother's attitude toward Indians;9. Cure for restoring hair lost from typhoid fever (Indian). Its success on Mrs. Carey;10. How to get curly hair;11. Why Indian women have black hair;12. Why the Micmacs have small families (learned from a magazine).

description_tesim: Side I An old miner named Sam - Delta Material in Dear Old Georgia. Jugo-Slavian Peasant Songs and Dances: Hop Saj Sasa - Croatia; Oj Mladosti Naj Milise Doba - Croatia; Sano Duso (sub-title: Vranjanka) - Serbia (Folkdancer records MH 3020; 78,B); Cacak - Serbia (Folkdancer Records MH 3022; 78,B); Krici Krici Ticek - Croatia; Poskakusa - Croatia; Kolo Kalendara - Croatia; Dance from Pirot - Servia (Sto Mi Omilelo); Makedonka - Serbia (Balkan 547; 78,A); Cujes Mala - Serbia; Kokonjeste - Serbia; Dajchovoto - Serbia; Serbian Medley: Poskok, Ti Momo, Djurdjevka, Igrale Se Delije. Side II Strike Music of American Labor: Hallelujah I'm a Bum (Greenway?); Pie in the Sky; Solidarity Forever; Union Maid - (a dub of the Almanac Singers record); Talking Union - (a dub of the Almanac Singers record); Sister Jenny's Turn to Throw the Ball; Ludlow Massacre - Woody Guthrie; Which Side - Almanacs; Join the NMU; Harry Sims; Fight for Union Recognition (tune: I've been workig on the railroad); The Boatmakers' Union is a No Good Union. Jane Fitzpatrick: An Ould Mon Came A-Courtin'; Oh Dear, What Shall Ee Dae; The Drowned Lover; Unnamed Tune.
Title from tape: Allen * Weide * Fitzpatrick
Performers: Jane Fitzpatrick, Sam (miner, last name unknown).

Content: Spanish Folklore; Lore of Holland. Side I: Comparisons and Proverbs; Comparisons and Proverbs. Side II: 10 Songs by Avelina Castillo [end Spanish Folk Speech]; Lore of Holland: Nichlas - song and customs associated with it; The Fifth of December - song; Santa Claus - song; Rhyme in Dutch - similar to Humpty Dumpty; Rhyme - about a little girl who went to the pawn shop and pawned her coat and then went to buy a lot of things; Discussion About People who Wear Wooden Shoes and Why; Song- about a mother's wish that her son will grow up to be a Captain; Pithain - song about the boy who brought in the Silver Fleet; Lullaby; Story about Hans and Fitche (Hansel and Gretal); National Holidays; Hide and Seek - game; Bim Bom Beya - counting out rhyme; Vie Vie Vye Vecht - counting out rhyme Green Pea Soup - how it is made; Ginger bread - how it is made; Meat Loaf - how it is made; Unbaked Apple Pie - how it is made; Frying Fish; The National Anthem of Holland
Title from tape: Spanish Folk Speech * Louise Demont

description_tesim: Content: WISHING ON A STAR - wishbone; candles on a birthday cake; LAST PIECE OF CAKE MEANS YOU'LL BE A WIDOW; WEDDINGS - throwing the boquet [sic]; rice, what to take on a honeymoon; IT'S BAD LUCK TO WEAR SOMETHING NEW TO A FUNERAL; TABOOS CONCERNING A WEDDING; SUPERSTITIONS ABOUT CLOTHING; IT'S BAD LUCK TO - break a mirror, sweep someone's foot, step over things, bring an old broom (or mop) into a new house, sweep after dark; SPECIAL MEALS ON NEW YEAR'S DAY; SUPERSTITIONS ABOUT CHRISTMAS TREES; WHAT YOU TKE INTO A NEW HOUSE; BACK OUT THE BACK DOOR; IF YOU GO THROUGH A DOOR WITH NO HINGES SAY "GOOD LUCK FOLLOW ME"; WHAT WILL HAPPEN IF YOUR PALM (EYE) ITCHES; EAR BURNS; QUIETING OR DISTURBING THE DEAD; COVERING MIRRORS AFTER A FUNERAL; HOW TO DISPOSE OF A PERSON'S POSSESSIONS AFTER HIS DEATH; THE DEAD INFLUENCE BABIES; AN ELDERLY PERSON IS NEVER SUPPOSED TO SLEEP WITH A BABY; IF YOU START SEWING SOMETHING ON YOUR PERIOD YOU SHOULD FINISH IT BEFORE THE PERIOD IS OVER; DON'T UNDERTAKE ANY NEW FINANCIAL PROBLEMS ON FRIDAY THE THIRTEENTH; 13 IS UNLUCKY BECAUSE IT DENOTES THE LAST SUPPER; A BLACK CAT ACROSS YOUR PATH IS UNLUCKY; IF YOU PUT YOUR CLOTHES ON INSIDE OUT IT IS BAD LUCK TO CHANGE THEM; IF DOGS BARK AT MIDNIGHT THERE WILL BE A STORM; DOGS BARK WHEN THEIR MASTER DIES; YOU ARE CLOSE TO DEATH WHEN YOU SNEEZE; MOTHER'S ACTIVITIES DURING PREGNANCY INFLUENCE CHILD'S TENDENCIES AFTER BIRTH; YOU CAN INFLUENCE CHILDREN BY TALKING TO THEM WHILE THEY SLEEP; STAINS COME OUT WITH VINEGAR AND SALT; SPILL SALT ON A WINE STAIN; KEEP ANIMALS AWAY FROM CHILDREN; IF YOU BREAK ANYTHING, THROW ALL THE PIECES AWAY TOGETHER IN A BAG; PIERCING EARS HELPS HEARING
Title from tape: M.* Lady Lore

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: This mural is the second to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: This mural was the first to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection
